topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The spreadsheet glowed on my monitor, a stark, unforgiving grid of numbers. I traced the figures for the last quarter, my gaze snagging on the disparity. Sarah’s compensation, listed plainly in the HR portal, was nearly double mine. A slow, cold knot tightened in my chest. I reread the figures, my fingers hovering over the mouse, unable to process the sheer imbalance. When Sarah walked past my cubicle, her easy laughter echoing faintly, I felt a sudden, sharp tightness around my throat. I quickly minimized the window, the screen’s brightness suddenly too harsh, and focused intently on the dust motes dancing in the afternoon sunlight slicing through the blinds.

[story 2]

"Just... wait a moment," I managed, my voice sounding thin and reedy even to my own ears. I watched Mark as he finalized his expense report, the digital confirmation popping up on his screen. The numbers swam before my eyes. It wasn't just a difference; it was a chasm. A strange hollowness began to spread through my limbs, making my hands feel clumsy and heavy. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the carpet. I walked toward the water cooler, gripping the cold metal carafe so tightly my knuckles were white. I took a long, shaky breath, trying to steady the erratic rhythm of my heart before forcing myself to return to my desk, the silence of the office suddenly amplifying the dull throb behind my eyes.

[story 3]

I stared at the pay stub, the ink blurring slightly under the intensity of my focus. The reality of the situation settled over me like a heavy blanket, suffocating. Every line item seemed to mock my own efforts. I picked up my pen, but the plastic felt slick and alien in my hand. A wave of profound disappointment washed over me, leaving a strange, numbing sensation in my stomach. I looked around the open-plan office, at the vibrant energy of my colleagues, and felt profoundly disconnected. I leaned back in my chair, the springs groaning beneath me, and let my head drop forward until my chin rested on my chest, trying desperately to make myself small enough to disappear into the beige partition wall.
